sourceMovie.title: Human
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Human for the same reason it broke through in 2015: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a documentary rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Yann Arthus-Bertrand keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Baraka
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Human clicked for you, Baraka (1992) is a strong next stop: both trade in documentary, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Saludos Amigos
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Human clicked for you, Saludos Amigos (1942) is a strong next stop: both trade in documentary, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Roger & Me
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Human clicked for you, Roger & Me (1989) is a strong next stop: both trade in documentary, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Human?
faq.answer: Try Baraka, Saludos Amigos, Roger & Me—each shares DNA with Human while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Human?
faq.answer: Human (2015) is best described as Documentary. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Human worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this documentary lane.
